course_info.title: Critical Thinking in the Digital Age

class_schedule.topic: What Is Critical Thinking and Why It Matters
learning_objectives: Define critical thinking and distinguish it from opinion
Identify the components of a well-reasoned argument
Explain why critical thinking is essential in information-rich environments
readings: Asking the Right Questions Ch. 1-2 by Browne & Keeley
The Necessity of Critical Thinking (Stanford Encyclopedia excerpt)

lecture_notes.introduction: Critical thinking is the disciplined process of actively analyzing, synthesizing, and evaluating information to guide belief and action. In an era of information overload, it is the single most important intellectual skill.
key_concepts: Definition of critical thinking vs. casual opinion
Claims, evidence, and reasoning
The difference between strong-sense and weak-sense critical thinking
Intellectual humility and open-mindedness
The Socratic method as a critical thinking tool
lecture_notes.demonstration: Class exercise: evaluate three claims about a current news topic and identify which are supported by evidence
setup_requirements: Access to course learning management system
Printed or digital copy of the day's news articles (provided)

review.value: Purposeful, self-regulatory judgment that results in interpretation, analysis, evaluation, and inference
Strong-sense applies standards fairly to all positions including one's own; weak-sense defends only pre-existing beliefs

learning_objectives: Identify at least ten common logical fallacies
Explain how cognitive biases distort reasoning
readings: Thinking, Fast and Slow Ch. 1-3 by Daniel Kahneman

lecture_notes.introduction: Logical fallacies are errors in reasoning that undermine an argument. Cognitive biases are systematic patterns of deviation from rational judgment. Knowing both is essential to evaluating information critically.
key_concepts: Ad hominem, straw man, and red herring fallacies
Appeal to authority, false dilemma, and slippery slope
Confirmation bias and anchoring bias
Availability heuristic and Dunning-Kruger effect
How to self-check for biases in your own reasoning
lecture_notes.demonstration: Fallacy identification game using political advertisements and social media posts
setup_requirements: Handout of 15 example arguments with fallacies (provided)

review.value: Attacking the person making the argument rather than the argument itself
The tendency to search for, interpret, and recall information that confirms pre-existing beliefs

learning_objectives: Apply the CRAAP test to evaluate source credibility
Distinguish between primary, secondary, and tertiary sources
readings: Web Literacy for Student Fact-Checkers by Mike Caulfield, Ch. 1-3

lecture_notes.introduction: Not all information is created equal. The ability to evaluate sources is the practical application of critical thinking to the information landscape.
key_concepts: CRAAP test: Currency, Relevance, Authority, Accuracy, Purpose
Lateral reading: checking what other sources say
Primary vs. secondary vs. tertiary sources
Misinformation (unintentionally false) vs. disinformation (intentionally false)
Fact-checking organizations and tools
lecture_notes.demonstration: Students evaluate five web sources on a controversial topic using the CRAAP test and compare ratings
setup_requirements: CRAAP test evaluation worksheet (provided in LMS)

review.value: A framework for evaluating sources across five dimensions: Currency, Relevance, Authority, Accuracy, Purpose
Instead of reading deeply into one source, open new tabs to verify claims by checking what other sources say about the same topic

learning_objectives: Distinguish between deductive and inductive reasoning
Evaluate the strength of evidence supporting a claim
readings: A Rulebook for Arguments by Anthony Weston, Ch. 1-4

lecture_notes.introduction: Building strong arguments is the constructive side of critical thinking. It requires clear claims, relevant evidence, and sound logical connections between them.
key_concepts: Claim, warrant, and evidence (Toulmin model)
Deductive reasoning: premises guarantee conclusion
Inductive reasoning: premises support but do not guarantee conclusion
Types of evidence: statistical, testimonial, analogical, anecdotal
Evaluating evidence quality and sufficiency
lecture_notes.demonstration: Students construct arguments for and against a campus policy using the Toulmin model

review.value: Claim (assertion), Data (evidence), Warrant (logical bridge), Backing (support for warrant), Qualifier (limits), Rebuttal (exceptions)
Deductive: if premises are true, conclusion must be true. Inductive: premises make conclusion probable but not certain

class_schedule.topic: Critical Analysis of Data and Statistics
learning_objectives: Identify misleading uses of statistics in media
Ask the right questions when presented with statistical claims
readings: How to Lie with Statistics by Darrell Huff (full book, short)
Calling Bullshit Ch. 5-6 by Bergstrom & West

lecture_notes.introduction: Data and statistics lend an aura of objectivity to claims, but they can be manipulated, cherry-picked, or misrepresented. Critical thinkers must be statistically literate.
key_concepts: Correlation vs. causation
Cherry-picking data and survivorship bias
Misleading graph techniques: truncated axes, 3D effects, selective scales
Sample size, representativeness, and margin of error
Questions to ask: Who collected it? How? What was excluded?
lecture_notes.demonstration: Students analyze five misleading graphs from news articles and identify the deceptive techniques
setup_requirements: Access to original data sources for comparison

review.value: Two variables moving together does not prove one causes the other; confounding variables or coincidence may explain the pattern
Truncated Y-axis, inconsistent intervals, cherry-picked time ranges, and 3D effects can all distort the visual impression of data

class_schedule.topic: Applying Critical Thinking to AI and Algorithmic Information
learning_objectives: Evaluate AI-generated content for accuracy and bias
Understand how recommendation algorithms create filter bubbles
Develop strategies for maintaining intellectual independence in algorithmic environments
readings: Weapons of Math Destruction Ch. 1-2 by Cathy O'Neil
How Search Engines Shape What We Know (Pew Research report)

lecture_notes.introduction: Algorithms now mediate much of the information we encounter. Critical thinking must extend to understanding how these systems select, filter, and present information to us.
key_concepts: How search engine algorithms rank information
Filter bubbles and echo chambers
AI hallucination and fabricated citations
Algorithmic bias in training data
Strategies: diverse sources, deliberate exposure to opposing views, verification
lecture_notes.demonstration: Students compare search results on a political topic across different search engines and analyze differences
setup_requirements: Access to at least three different search engines
AI chatbot access for testing hallucination examples

review.value: Algorithms personalize content based on past behavior, progressively narrowing the range of information a user encounters
AI systems can generate plausible-sounding but entirely fabricated information, including fake citations and statistics

questions.question: Identify the logical fallacy in the following argument and explain why it is fallacious: 'Professor Smith's research on climate change cannot be trusted because she drives an SUV.'
questions.answer: This is an ad hominem fallacy. It attacks Professor Smith's personal behavior rather than addressing the quality or evidence of her research. The validity of scientific research is determined by methodology, data, and peer review, not by the personal habits of the researcher. Even if there is hypocrisy in her behavior, it does not invalidate her research findings.

rubric.key: Notes that personal behavior does not invalidate research

questions.question: Explain the CRAAP test and apply it to evaluate a Wikipedia article as a source for an academic paper. Would you use it? Why or why not?
questions.answer: The CRAAP test evaluates Currency (when was it updated), Relevance (does it address the topic), Authority (who wrote it), Accuracy (is it verifiable), and Purpose (why does it exist). A Wikipedia article has mixed scores: it is often current and relevant, but authority is weak because anyone can edit it, accuracy varies by article, and its purpose is general reference rather than scholarly analysis. It should not be cited directly in academic papers but can be used as a starting point to find primary sources through its references section.

rubric.key: Correctly defines all five CRAAP criteria
Applies each criterion to Wikipedia specifically

questions.question: Using the Toulmin model, construct an argument for or against requiring media literacy education in high schools. Label each component of your argument.
questions.answer: Claim: Media literacy should be required in high schools. Data: A Stanford study found that 82% of middle school students could not distinguish between sponsored content and news articles. Warrant: Students who cannot evaluate information sources are vulnerable to manipulation and make poorly informed decisions. Backing: Democratic societies depend on citizens who can critically assess the information they encounter. Qualifier: While no curriculum can eliminate all susceptibility to misinformation. Rebuttal: Some argue parents should teach this, but many parents lack these skills themselves.

rubric.key: All six Toulmin components present and labeled

questions.question: A news headline reads: 'Study shows students who eat breakfast score 20% higher on exams.' What critical questions should you ask before accepting this claim? List at least four.
questions.answer: Critical questions: (1) Is this correlation or causation? Students who eat breakfast may also have more stable home environments that contribute to academic success. (2) What was the sample size and how were participants selected? A small or non-representative sample weakens the finding. (3) Were confounding variables controlled, such as socioeconomic status, sleep quality, or prior academic performance? (4) Who funded the study? A cereal company funding breakfast research has a financial interest in positive results. (5) What does '20% higher' mean exactly? Is it 20 percentage points or 20 percent relative improvement?

rubric.key: Identifies confounding variables or funding bias
At least four distinct critical questions

major_assignments.title: Critical Analysis of a Viral Claim
major_assignments.description: Select a viral claim or widely shared piece of information from social media or news. Conduct a rigorous critical analysis using the tools and frameworks from this course. Your analysis must trace the claim to its original source, evaluate the evidence, identify any fallacies or biases, and present a well-reasoned conclusion about the claim's validity.
objectives: Apply source evaluation techniques to a real-world claim
Identify logical fallacies and cognitive biases in popular discourse
Construct a well-reasoned argument supported by evidence
Demonstrate media literacy and fact-checking skills
deliverables: Source evaluation table applying CRAAP test to at least 5 sources
Annotated bibliography of all sources consulted
Visual timeline showing how the claim spread across platforms
Conclusion with confidence rating and reasoning

criteria: At least 5 sources evaluated using CRAAP test
Original source of claim identified and traced
Cognitive biases recognized in claim's spread
Appropriate qualifiers and acknowledgment of limitations
Visual timeline is informative and well-designed
Thorough and rigorous analysis demonstrating all course concepts
Original source traced and multiple sources evaluated systematically
Well-constructed argument with fair treatment of counterarguments
Professional presentation with clear, compelling writing

participation_and_discussion.description: Active participation in class discussions, debates, and fallacy identification exercises

quizzes.description: Regular assessment of critical thinking concepts and application

weekly_reflections.description: Short weekly reflections applying critical thinking to current events

major_analysis_paper.description: In-depth critical analysis of a viral claim demonstrating all course skills

final_debate.description: Structured debate on a controversial topic demonstrating evidence-based argumentation

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is essential for a discussion-based course. More than 3 unexcused absences will lower your final grade by one letter grade.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late work is accepted up to 3 days with a 10% daily penalty. Extensions must be requested at least 24 hours before the deadline.
course_policies.collaboration: Discussions and debates are collaborative by nature. Written assignments must be individual work with proper attribution of all sources and ideas.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Critical thinking demands intellectual honesty. Plagiarism, fabricated sources, or misrepresented evidence will result in a zero on the assignment and possible course failure.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need internet access for research and source evaluation. A laptop or tablet is recommended for in-class activities.
